blocks.content: She didn't go home right away.

She walked, instead. The evening was the right kind — mild, the light that kind of late gold it goes in May when the day is done but hasn't fully admitted it yet. She didn't have a direction. She just walked, the way she sometimes did when something large was settling and she needed her body to be moving so her mind could be still.

She went past the pharmacy. Past the cafe with the green awning that she'd texted Ethan about, which felt like something that had happened in a different chapter of the week but had also been this morning. The rubric. The thriving plant.
She walked until the streets opened out and she was at the edge of the park.
She hadn't planned it. Her feet had planned it. Her body had done what her mind had been circling since the morning she'd realized that she loved him — had brought her back to where it started.

The park in the evening was a different place than the park in the morning. The morning version was active, full of the purposeful movement of people trying to get somewhere. The evening version was slower. The joggers were less hurried. The families were winding down. The dogs were pulling their owners toward the grass with the unhurried authority of beings who understand that there is no agenda more important than this particular smell, right now.
She walked through it. She didn't have to think about where she was going.
